content:
The autopilot is great but true software engineers know exactly when to grab the steering wheel.

6 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Fotis Fotopoulos on UnsplashYou are staring at a screen full of errors and your coffee has gone completely cold. Your patience is depleted and you are probably ready to throw your laptop out the nearest window. Sound familiar? Right now you are navigating a landscape that feels completely broken. We are caught in this weird limbo where seasoned developers tell you to memorize complex algorithms while new tools churn out hundreds of lines of functional software in seconds. So what are you actually supposed to do? Do you spend weeks learning how grid layouts work or do you just prompt a language model to build your user interface?
The brutal reality of our industry right now is that artificial intelligence acts like a bulldozer. If you do not know how to act as the architect you are just going to flatten your own career. There is a new term floating around our circles and we call it vibe coding. It happens when a developer refuses to actually read the output their assistant generates. They just blindly prompt and copy and paste and ship features based entirely on the feeling that the software seems to be working. We might eventually reach a future where nobody has to look at the underlying logic but we are absolutely not there yet. I have watched junior developers completely rely on these models and I have seen the absolute mess it creates in a production environment. If you are not meticulously crafting your prompts the machine is going to generate sloppy and horrifying logic. It leaves strange values scattered everywhere and duplicates entire blocks instead of creating reusable components.
You are probably thinking that dealing with those little messes is just the price of moving fast. Here is the thing about that assumption. Moving fast in the wrong direction just gets you lost much quicker. Before you let the machine do the heavy lifting you must figure out how to lift the weight yourself. Take a simple interface button that has an icon and some text and needs to be perfectly centered. Do you actually know how to build that by hand? Do you know the correct layout system to use or understand the difference between aligning items and justifying content? If you cannot center a simple component manually you should never ask a machine to do it for you.
You have to earn the right to automate the boring stuff by understanding how it actually works. Spend the time to code these things by hand at least once. Once you understand the fundamental layout mechanics you should absolutely use these tools to generate that tedious styling. But the biggest mistake I made early on was thinking that these tools replaced the need to study system architecture. It is exactly the opposite. Knowing design patterns is what makes you a brilliant prompter. When you build software you are not just stacking bricks. You are designing a system that needs to be modular and extendable. This means you need to sit down and read the boring and heavy books. Dive into system architecture. Learn the established design patterns. Understand what an abstract factory is or how a builder pattern operates.
I remember working on a real time application a few years back. The server needed to update dozens of times every single second to track moving elements. My memory usage spiked so hard that the garbage collector would freeze the entire application causing massive lag spikes. It was a complete disaster. But because I had studied system architecture I knew about a specific design pattern called an object pool. Instead of constantly creating and destroying objects in memory an object pool reuses a set of preallocated objects to bypass that exact bottleneck. Because I knew the exact vocabulary I went to my code editor and prompted it to refactor the server to use an object pool pattern. The tool knocked the feature out perfectly on the first try. If you do not know what an object pool is how would you ever know to ask the machine to build one?
Read the books and learn the patterns because it makes your prompts infinitely more powerful. I see beginners doing the exact opposite all the time. They will sit in front of their monitor for five hours violently sweating over a basic configuration error or a missing bracket. Please stop doing this. There is an inverse rule of thumb here which dictates that if the risk is low you should let the machine handle it. If you misconfigured a file or passed the wrong property into a function do not burn half your workday trying to play detective. These are massive time sinks that provide zero educational value to you. Paste the error into your prompt and let it fix the syntax and move on.
However there is a massive catch to this workflow. When the tool gives you the fix you must read the explanation. You have to ask yourself how this fixed the bug and what was causing the issue in the first place. Think about how to write cleaner logic to prevent this next time. If you just copy the solution and ignore the underlying reason you are not learning anything. You are just acting as a highly paid typist. As you rely on these tools to generate features your project is going to grow incredibly fast. Suddenly your weekend side project is spanning dozens of folders and thousands of lines of code.
Here is a quick test to see if you are actually an engineer or just someone holding on for dear life. Can you find a specific component in your codebase in under five seconds? If I asked you to add a new refresh button to a specific panel in your application do you know exactly which file to open? Can you quickly navigate your own creation? When you keep asking a language model to add features it often patches bugs directly where it finds them. Let us say you find a bug in a modal and you prompt the machine and it fixes the modal. That sounds great until you realize you have that exact same bug on five different pages because the logic was never abstracted into a centralized and reusable component. Your project becomes a fragmented and duplicated nightmare.
You will reach a point where the language model can no longer solve your problems because the codebase is too messy. You must be the one who navigates the jungle and understands the structure and guides the machine to centralize and refactor everything. Let us talk about getting a job right now because it is weird. The interviewing landscape is completely fractured. You will walk into one company and they will explicitly ban you from using assistants during the technical screen. The next day you will interview at a startup that throws you into an environment with an AI assistant and judges you entirely on how well you use it. It feels like a trap. You are afraid to ask questions because you think the hiring manager will assume you are an idiot.
Here is how you survive. Become the conductor and not the instrument. If they allow you to use tools they are watching your instincts. Do you immediately start wildly prompting or do you open a file to outline the system requirements and write out the edge cases first? They want to see if you understand the underlying runtime. If you are building a server application do you actually know how the event loop works? These deep dive questions exist to prove that when the machine inevitably hallucinates or fails you have the foundational knowledge to step in and fix the mess.
